quotes.verbatim_quote:
The ii V lis the most important progression in jazz, and therefore it’s the best
place to start when learning jazz guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the foundational pedagogical premise of the entire method — that the ii V I is the organizing principle from which all other learning flows.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
To begin, here are four chords that fit over the ii V | progression, with the Cmaj7
and Cmaj9 being two colors of the Cmaj7 sound.

Start by playing these chords on your own, memorize them, and then add in a
metronome when ready.

From there, get these chords onto the backing track when you can, play it as
written, then start to play with the rhythms and picking from there.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage introduces the core chord vocabulary (Dm7, G7, Cmaj7, Cmaj9) and gives the explicit learning sequence: solo practice → metronome → backing track → rhythmic variation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first rhythm is called the Freddie Green rhythm, as it was made famous by
Count Basie guitarist Freddie Green.

The rhythm is all quarter notes, meaning you play one chord per beat.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the Freddie Green comping rhythm by name and its structural rule — one chord per beat in quarter notes — giving the student a labeled, reproducible technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
To give it a more authentic Freddie sound, accent the 24 and 4" chords/beats in
each bar.

This imitates the hi-hat and helps you bring a higher level of swing to the rhythm.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a specific performance rule — accent beats 2 and 4 — that distinguishes authentic swing feel from mere chord-on-every-beat strumming.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Charleston rhythm, as it’s based on the dance
from the 1920s.

This rhythm places the first chord on beat 1 of each bar, then the second chord is
on the & of 2, between beats 2 and 3 in each bar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the Charleston rhythm by name and gives its precise placement rule (beat 1 and the & of 2), making it a teachable, repeatable rhythmic pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
From there, mix the Freddie Green and Charleston rhythms together to expand
on this idea in your comping.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the student a compositional rule for developing their own comping — combining the two named rhythms rather than playing either one verbatim — marking the transition from imitation to improvised application.
